Texto Gabarito:
Isotopes of hydrogen
Article Talk
From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Hydrogen (1H) has three naturally occurring isotopes: 1H, 2H, and 3H. 1H and 2H are stable, while 3H has a half-life of 12.32 years.[4][nb 1] Heavier isotopes also exist; all are synthetic and have a half-life of less than 1 zeptosecond (10−21 s).[5][6]
Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: 2H is deuterium[7] and 3H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols 2H and 3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.[9] 1H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

Texto Gabarito:
Isotopes of hydrogen
Article Talk
From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Hydrogen (1H) has three naturally occurring isotopes: 1H, 2H, and 3H. 1H and 2H are stable, while 3H has a half-life of 12.32 years.[4][nb 1] Heavier isotopes also exist; all are synthetic and have a half-life of less than 1 zeptosecond (10−21 s).[5][6]
Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: 2H is deuterium[7] and 3H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols 2H and 3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.[9] 1H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

Isotopes of hydrogen
Article Talk
From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Hydrogen (1H) has three naturally occurring isotopes: 1H, 2H, and 3H. 1H and 2H are stable, while 3H has a half-life of 12.32 years.[4][nb 1] Heavier isotopes also exist; all are synthetic and have a half-life of less than 1 zeptosecond (10−21 s).[5][6]
Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: 2H is deuterium[7] and 3H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols 2H and 3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.[9] 1H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

Hydrogen (1H) has three naturally occurring isotopes:
1H, 2H, and 3H. 1H and 2H are stable, while 3H has a half-life of 12.32 years. [4][nb 1] Heavier isotopes also exist; all are synthetic and have a half-life of less than 1 zeptosecond (10−²¹ s). . [5][6]

Heavier isotopes, such as deuterium and tritium, are also allowed. The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuteron and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols 2H and 3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetical sorting of chemical formulas. [9] symbols 2H and 3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetical sorting of chemical formulas. [10]
During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy isotopes were given [names], but such names are rarely used today.

Hydrogen (1H) has three naturally occurring isotopes: 1H, 2H, and 3H. 1H and 2H are stable, while 3H has a half-life of 12.32 years.[4][nb 1] Heavier isotopes also exist; all are synthetic and have a half-life of less than 1 zeptosecond (10−21 s).[5][6]
Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: 2H is deuterium[7] and 3H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols 2H and 3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.[9] 1H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

ページ ノート

出典: フリー百科事典『ウィキペディア（Wikipedia）』
水素（H、標準原子量: 1.008[1]）には天然に1H、2Hおよび3Hの3つの同位体がある。その他には非常に不安定な核種（4Hから7H）が実験室で合成されているが、天然には全く存在しない[2][3]。
水素は今日ではその同位体に特殊な名前が使われている唯一の元素である（放射線の研究の初期には、放射性同位体に様々な名前が与えられてきた〔en:List of elements by symbol#Symbols for named isotopes〕。しかし、そのような名前は今日ではめったに使われない）。元素記号DとT（2Hと3Hの代替）が重水素と三重水素に対して用いられることがある。IUPACはこれらの記号を慣用として記載しているが推奨はしていない。

\subtext 同论 汉 润
大陆简体

维基百科，自由的百科全书

氢 （原子量：1.00794（7））共有7个已知同位素，质量数介于1-7之间，其中有2个是稳定的，其他都具有放射性。天然存在的氢同位素有3个，分别是稳定剂（¹H）、氘（²H）和具放射性的氚（³H），其中³H在自然界中仅痕量存在。为宇宙射线所产生。另外四个同位素（³H到³H）都不知道在实验室制造出来，且半衰期都少于10-21 秒，极为不稳定。氢唯一一个天然同位素各拥有不同名称的元素。虽然在一些有关放射性的早期文献中，一些属于自然界中三大衰变链的放射性核素也自己专属的名称和化学符号，但是今日已经鲜少使用了。

氢（原子量：1.00794(7)）共有7个已知同位素，质量数介于1-7之间，其中有2个是稳定的，其他都具有放射性。天然存在的氢同位素有3个，分别是稳定的（1H）、（2H）和具放射性的（3H），其中3H在自然界中仅痕量存在，为宇宙射线所产生。另外四个同位素（4H到7H）都不出现在自然界中，只有在实验室制造出来过，且半衰期都少于10-21秒，极为不稳定。氢是唯一一个天然同位素各拥有不同名称的元素。虽然在一些有关放射性的早期文献中，一些属于自然界中三大衰变链的放射性核素也有自己专属的名称和化学符号，但是今日已经鲜少使用了。

Texto Gabarito:
氢的同位素 [编辑]

条目 讨论 汉 漢 大陆简体

维基百科，自由的百科全书
氢（原子量：1.00794(7)）共有7个已知同位素，质量数介于1-7之间，其中有2个是稳定的，其他都具有放射性。天然存在的氢同位素有3个，分别是稳定的（1H）、（2H）和具放射性的（3H），其中3H在自然界中仅痕量存在，为宇宙射线所产生。另外四个同位素（4H到7H）都不出现在自然界中，只有在实验室制造出来过，且半衰期都少于10-21秒，极为不稳定。氢是唯一一个天然同位素各拥有不同名称的元素。虽然在一些有关放射性的早期文献中，一些属于自然界中三大衰变链的放射性核素也有自己专属的名称和化学符号，但是今日已经鲜少使用了。

Texto Gabarito:
Isotopes of hydrogen
Article Talk
From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Hydrogen (1H) has three naturally occurring isotopes: 1H, 2H, and 3H. 1H and 2H are stable, while 3H has a half-life of 12.32 years.[4][nb 1] Heavier isotopes also exist; all are synthetic and have a half-life of less than 1 zeptosecond (10−21 s).[5][6]
Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: 2H is deuterium[7] and 3H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols 2H and 3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.[9] 1H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

Hydrogen (1H) has three naturally occurring isotopes: 1H, 2H, and 3H. 1H and 2H are stable, while 3H has a half-life of 12.32 years.[4][nb 1] Heavier isotopes also exist; all are synthetic and have a half-life of less than 1 zeptosecond (10−21 s).[5][6]
Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: 2H is deuterium[7] and 3H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols 2H and 3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.[9] 1H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

Isotopes of hydrogen
Article Talk
From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Hydrogen (1H) has three naturally occurring isotopes: 1H, 2H, and 3H. 1H and 2H are stable, while 3H has a half-life of 12.32 years.[4][nb 1] Heavier isotopes also exist; all are synthetic and have a half-life of less than 1 zeptosecond (10−21 s).[5][6]
Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: 2H is deuterium[7] and 3H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols 2H and 3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.[9] 1H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

出典: フリー百科事典『ウィキペディア（Wikipedia）』
水素（H、標準原子量: 1.008[1]）には天然に1H、2Hおよび3Hの3つの同位体がある。その他には非常に不安定な核種（4Hから7H）が実験室で合成されているが、天然には全く存在しない[2][3]。
水素は今日ではその同位体に特殊な名前が使われている唯一の元素である（放射線の研究の初期には、放射性同位体に様々な名前が与えられてきた〔en:List of elements by symbol#Symbols for named isotopes〕。しかし、そのような名前は今日ではめったに使われない）。元素記号DとT（2Hと3Hの代替）が重水素と三重水素に対して用いられることがある。IUPACはこれらの記号を慣用として記載しているが推奨はしていない。

Texto Gabarito:
水素の同位体
ページ ノート

出典: フリー百科事典『ウィキペディア（Wikipedia）』
水素（H、標準原子量: 1.008[1]）には天然に1H、2Hおよび3Hの3つの同位体がある。その他には非常に不安定な核種（4Hから7H）が実験室で合成されているが、天然には全く存在しない[2][3]。
水素は今日ではその同位体に特殊な名前が使われている唯一の元素である（放射線の研究の初期には、放射性同位体に様々な名前が与えられてきた〔en:List of elements by symbol#Symbols for named isotopes〕。しかし、そのような名前は今日ではめったに使われない）。元素記号DとT（2Hと3Hの代替）が重水素と三重水素に対して用いられることがある。IUPACはこれらの記号を慣用として記載しているが推奨はしていない。

水素（H、標準原子量: 1.008[1]）には天然に¹H、²Hおよび³Hの3つの同一位体がある。その他には非常に不安定な核種（⁴Hから⁷H）が実験室で合成されているが、天然には全く存在しない(2][3]。  
水素は今日ではその同位体に特殊な名前が使われている唯一の元素である（放射線の研究での初期には、放射性同位体に様々な名前が与えられてきた (en:List of elements by symbol#Symbols for named isotopes)〕。しかし、そのような名前は今日ではめったに使われない(元素記号DとT （²Hと³Hの代替）が重水素と三重水素に対して用いられることがある)。IUPACはこれらの記号を慣用とともに記載しているが推薦作っていない。

（放射線の研究の）対応不明記載。

氢（原子量：1.00794(7)）共有7个已知同位素，质量数介于1-7之间，其中有2个是稳定的，其他都具有放射性。天然存在的氢同位素有3个，分别是稳定的（1H）、（2H）和具放射性的（3H），其中3H在自然界中仅痕量存在，为宇宙射线所产生。另外四个同位素（4H到7H）都不出现在自然界中，只有在实验室制造出来过，且半衰期都少于10-21秒，极为不稳定。氢是唯一一个天然同位素各拥有不同名称的元素。虽然在一些有关放射性的早期文献中，一些属于自然界中三大衰变链的放射性核素也有自己专属的名称和化学符号，但是今日已经鲜少使用了。

条目      讨论    汉 漢 大陆简体

维基百科，自由的百科全书

氢（原子量：1.00794(7)）共有7个已知同位素，质量数介于1-7之间，其中有2个是稳定的，其他都具有放射性。天然存在的氢同位素有3个，分别是稳定态的氶（¹H）、氶（²H）和具放射性态的氶（³H），其中³H在自然界中仅痕量存在，为宇宙射线所产生。另外四個同位素（⁴H到⁷H）都不出现在自然界中，只有在实验室制造出来过，且半衰期都少于10⁻²¹秒，极为不穩定。氢是唯一一个天然同位素各拥有不同名称的元素。虽然在一些有关放射性的早期文献中，一些属于自然界中三大衰变链的放射性核素也有自己专属的名称和化学符号，但是今日已经鲜少使用了。

辐射性核素也有自己专属的名称和化学符号，但是今日已经鲜少使用了。

氢（原子量：1.00794(7)）共有7个已知同位素，质量数介于1-7之间，其中有2个是稳定的的，其他都具有放射性。天然存在的氢同位素有3个，分别是稳定的氙（^1H）、氆（^2H）和具放射性的氆（^3H），其中^3H在自然界中仅痕量存在，为宇宙射线所产生的。另外四个同位素（^4H到7H）都不出现在自然界中，只有在实验室制造出来过，且半衰期都少于10⁻²¹秒，极为不稳定性。氢是唯一一个天然同位素各拥有不同名称的元素。虽然在一些有关放射性的早期文献中，一些属于自然界中三大衰变链的放射性核素也有自己专属的名称和化学符号，但是今日已经鲜少使用了。

Texto Gabarito:
Isotopes of hydrogen
Article Talk
From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Hydrogen (1H) has three naturally occurring isotopes: 1H, 2H, and 3H. 1H and 2H are stable, while 3H has a half-life of 12.32 years.[4][nb 1] Heavier isotopes also exist; all are synthetic and have a half-life of less than 1 zeptosecond (10−21 s).[5][6]
Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: 2H is deuterium[7] and 3H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols 2H and 3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.[9] 1H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

Hydrogen (1H) has three naturally occurring isotopes: 1H, 2H, and 3H. 1H and 2H are stable, while 3H has a half-life of 12.32 years.[4][nb 1] Heavier isotopes also exist; all are synthetic and have a half-life of less than 1 zeptosecond (10−21 s).[5][6]

Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: 2H is deuterium[7] and 3H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols 2H and 3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.[9] 1H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

Hydrogen (1H) has three naturally occurring isotopes: 1H, 2H, and 3H. 1H and 2H are stable, while 3H has a half-life of 12.32 years.[4][nb 1] Heavier isotopes also exist; all are synthetic and have a half-life of less than 1 zeptosecond (10−21 s).5][6]

Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: 2H is deuterium[7] and 3H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols 2H and 3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetical sorting of chemical formulas.[9] 1H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

Hydrogen (1H) has three naturally occurring isotopes: 1H, 2H, and 3H. 1H and 2H are stable, while 3H has a half-life of 12.32 years.[4][nb 1] Heavier isotopes also exist; all are synthetic and have a half-life of less than 1 zeptosecond (10−21 s).[5][6]
Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: 2H is deuterium[7] and 3H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols 2H and 3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.[9] 1H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: 2H is deuterium[7] and 3H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols 2H and 3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.[9] 1H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

Hydrogen (_1H) has three naturally occurring isotopes: ^1H, ^2H, and ^3H. ^1H and ^2H are stable, while ^3H has a half-life of 12.32 years.[4][nb 1] Heavier isotopes also exist; all are synthetic and have a half-life of less than 1 zeptosecond (10−21 s). [5][6]

Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: ^2H is deuterium[7] and ^3H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols ^2H and ^3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetical sorting of chemical formulas.[9] ^1H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

Hydrogen (₁H) has three naturally occurring isotopes: ₁H, ₂H, and ³H. ₁H and ₂H are stable, while ³H has a half-life of 12.32 years.⁴[nb 1] Heavier isotopes also exist; all are synthetic and have a half-life of less than 1 zeptosecond (10⁻²¹ s).⁵[6]

Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: ₂H is deuterium⁷ and ³H is tritium.⁸ The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols ²H and ³H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.⁹ ₁H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.¹⁰ During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

Texto Gabarito:
水素の同位体
ページ ノート

出典: フリー百科事典『ウィキペディア（Wikipedia）』
水素（H、標準原子量: 1.008[1]）には天然に1H、2Hおよび3Hの3つの同位体がある。その他には非常に不安定な核種（4Hから7H）が実験室で合成されているが、天然には全く存在しない[2][3]。
水素は今日ではその同位体に特殊な名前が使われている唯一の元素である（放射線の研究の初期には、放射性同位体に様々な名前が与えられてきた〔en:List of elements by symbol#Symbols for named isotopes〕。しかし、そのような名前は今日ではめったに使われない）。元素記号DとT（2Hと3Hの代替）が重水素と三重水素に対して用いられることがある。IUPACはこれらの記号を慣用として記載しているが推奨はしていない。

水素は今日ではその同位体に特殊な名前が使われている唯一の元素である（放射線の研究の初期には、放射性同位体に様々な名前が与えられてきた（en:List of elements by symbol#Symbols for named isotopes）。しかし、そのような名前は今日ではめったに使われない）。元素記号DとT（²Hと³Hの代替）が重水素と三重水素に対して用いられることがある。IUPACはこれらの記号を慣用として記載しているが推奨はしていない。

氢的同位素 [编辑]

条目 讨论 汉 漢 大陆简体

维基百科，自由的百科全书
氢（原子量：1.00794(7)）共有7个已知同位素，质量数介于1-7之间，其中有2个是稳定的，其他都具有放射性。天然存在的氢同位素有3个，分别是稳定的（1H）、（2H）和具放射性的（3H），其中3H在自然界中仅痕量存在，为宇宙射线所产生。另外四个同位素（4H到7H）都不出现在自然界中，只有在实验室制造出来过，且半衰期都少于10-21秒，极为不稳定。氢是唯一一个天然同位素各拥有不同名称的元素。虽然在一些有关放射性的早期文献中，一些属于自然界中三大衰变链的放射性核素也有自己专属的名称和化学符号，但是今日已经鲜少使用了。

Isotopes of hydrogen
Article Talk
From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Hydrogen (1H) has three naturally occurring isotopes: 1H, 2H, and 3H. 1H and 2H are stable, while 3H has a half-life of 12.32 years.[4][nb 1] Heavier isotopes also exist; all are synthetic and have a half-life of less than 1 zeptosecond (10−21 s).[5][6]
Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: 2H is deuterium[7] and 3H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols 2H and 3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.[9] 1H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

Hydrogen (1H) has three naturally occurring isotopes: 1H, 2H, and 3H. 1H and 2H are stable, while 3H has a half-life of 12.32 years.[4][nb 1] Heavier isotopes also exist; all are synthetic and have a half-life of less than 1 zeptosecond (10−21 s).[5][6]
Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: 2H is deuterium[7] and 3H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols 2H and 3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.[9] 1H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: 2H is deuterium[7] and 3H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols 2H and 3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.[9] 1H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: 2H is deuterium[7] and 3H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols 2H and 3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.[9] 1H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: 2H is deuterium[7] and 3H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols 2H and 3H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.[9] 1H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Hydrogen (₁H) has three naturally occurring isotopes: ¹H, ²H, and ³H. ¹H and ²H are stable, while ³H has a half-life of 12.32 years.[4][nb 1] Heavier isotopes also exist; all are synthetic and have a half-life of less than 1 zeptosecond (10⁻²¹ s).[5][6]

Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: ²H is deuterium[7] and ³H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols ²H and ³H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.[9] ¹H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radio isotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: 2H is deuterium[7] and 3H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols<sup>2</sup>H and<sup>3</sup>H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.[9] 1H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

Hydrogen is the only element whose isotopes have different names that remain in common use today: ²H is deuterium[7] and ³H is tritium.[8] The symbols D and T are sometimes used for deuterium and tritium; IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) accepts said symbols, but recommends the standard isotopic symbols ²H and ³H, to avoid confusion in alphabetic sorting of chemical formulas.[9] ¹H, with no neutrons, may be called protium to disambiguate.[10] During the early study of radioactivity, some other heavy radioisotopes were given names, but such names are rarely used today.

水素（H、標準原子量: 1.008[1]）には天然に1H、2Hおよび3Hの3つの同位体がある。その他には非常に不安定な核種（4Hから7H）が実験室で合成されているが、天然には全く存在しない[2][3]。

ページ ノート 

出典: フリ百科事典『ウィキペディア(Wikipedia)』

水素（H、標準原子量: 1.008[1]）には天然に¹H、₂Hおよび₃Hの3つの同位体がある。その他には非常に不安定な核種（⁴Hから⁷H）が実験室で合成されているが、天然には全く存在しない[2][3]。

水素は今日ではその同位体に特殊な名前が使われている唯一の元素である（放射線の研究の初期には、放射性同位体に様々な名前が与えられてきた（en:List of elements by symbol#Symbols for named isotopes）。しかし、そのような名前は今日ではめったに使われない）。元素記号DとT（₂Hと₃Hの代替）が重水素と三重水素に対して用いられることがある。IUPACはこれらの記号を慣用として記載しているが推奨はしていない。

氢（原子量：1.00794(7)）共有7个已知同位素，质量数介于1-7之间，其中有2个是稳定的，其他都具有放射性。天然存在的氢同位素有3个，分别是稳定的（1H）、（2H）和具放射性的（3H），其中3H在自然界中仅痕量存在，为宇宙射线所产生。另外四个同位素（4H到7H）都不出现在自然界中，只有在实验室制造出来过，且半衰期都少于10-21秒，极为不稳定。氢是唯一一个天然同位素各拥有不同名称的元素。虽然在一些有关放射性的早期文献中，一些属于自然界中三大衰变链的放射性核素也有自己专属的名称和化学符号，但是今日已经鲜少使用了。

氢（原子量：1.00794(7)）共有7个已知同位素，质量数介于1-7之间，其中有2个是稳定的，其他都具有放射性。天然存在的氢同位素有3个，分别是稳定的氕（¹H）、气（²H）和具放射性的氚（³H)，其中³H在自然界中仅痕存在，为宇宙射线所产生。另外四个同位素（⁴H到⁷H）都不出现在自然界中，只有在实验室制造出来过，且半衰期都少于10⁻²¹秒，极为不稳定。氢是唯一一个天然同位素各拥有不同名称的元素。虽然在一些有关放射性的早期文献中，一些属于自然界中三大衰变链的放射性核素也有自己专属的名称和化学符号，但是今日已经鲜少使用了。

氢（原子量：1.00794(7)）共有7个已知同位素，质量数介于1－7之间，其中有2个是稳定的，其他都具有放射性。天然存在的氢同位素有3个，分别是稳定的氕（1H）、氚（2H）和具放射性的氚（3H），其中3H在自然界中仅痕量存在，为宇宙射线所产生。另外四个同位素（4H到7H）都不出现在自然界中，只有在实验室制造出来过，且半衰期都少于10⁻²¹秒，极为不稳定。氢是唯一一个天然同位素各拥有不同名称的元素。虽然在一些有关放射性的早期文献中，一些属于自然界中三大衰变链的放射性核素也有自己专属的名称和化学符号，但是今日已经鲜少使用了。

氢（原子量：1.00794(7)）共有7个已知同位素，质量数介于1-7之间，其中有2个是稳定的，其他都具有放射性。天然存在的氢同位素有3个，分别是稳定的氕（¹H）、氚（²H）和具放射性的氚（³H），其中³H在自然界中仅痕量存在，为宇宙射线所产生。另外四个同位素（⁴H到⁷H）都不出现在自然界中，只有在实验室制造出来过，且半衰期都少于10⁻²¹ 秒，极为不稳定。氢是唯一一个天然同位素各拥有不同名称的元素。虽然在一些有关放射性的早期文献中，一些属于自然界中三大衰变链的放射性核素也有自己专属的名称和化学符号，但是今日已经鲜少了。
